questions.question: Is a formal TPP defined and aligned with regulatory and commercial goals?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluation of TPP existence and alignment. For biologics, TPP must address biologic-specific elements: immunogenicity section in labeling, dosing regimen (IV/SC, flat vs. weight-based, step-up for bispecifics), target expression-based patient selection, and combination product (device) considerations.
questions.rationale: For biologics, the TPP defines product characteristics that are fundamentally different from SM — administration route/device, immunogenicity profile, target expression-based patient selection, and for bispecifics, CRS management requirements. These must be addressed in the TPP from the outset as they shape every downstream decision.

questions.question: Does the clinical development plan (CDP) map to the TPP with clear inflection points?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluation of CDP-TPP alignment. For biologics, inflection points include: target engagement confirmation (RO/PD), immunogenicity assessment timing, comparability PK bridging after process changes, and for bispecifics, CRS risk assessment at each dose level.
questions.rationale: Biologic CDPs have additional inflection points not present in SM development — immunogenicity impact assessment, CMC comparability bridging (if process changes occur during pivotal phase), and for bispecifics, dose optimisation decisions that must balance efficacy with CRS risk.

questions.question: Are the indications well-justified in terms of unmet need and path to registration?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluation of indication justification. For biologics, includes assessment of whether the target is validated in the indication (target expression data), whether the MOA is differentiated from existing biologic therapies, and whether accelerated approval pathways (common for oncology biologics) are viable.
questions.rationale: Biologic indication selection must consider target validation in the disease population — a mAb against a target not expressed in the disease tissue will fail regardless of clinical design. Many oncology biologics (especially bispecifics) pursue accelerated approval, requiring indication selection compatible with single-arm or early-endpoint strategies.

questions.question: Are the development goals feasible within the competitive and payer landscape?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluation of competitive feasibility. For biologics, includes assessment of the biologic competitive landscape (approved mAbs, emerging bispecifics, ADCs, cell therapies), biosimilar entry timing for competitors, and payer willingness to pay for biologic-class pricing. For bispecifics, assessment of whether the bispecific offers clear advantages over mAb combination therapy.
questions.rationale: Biologic competitive dynamics differ from SM — biosimilar entry after BPCIA exclusivity, rapid expansion of bispecific and ADC classes, and high price points requiring strong differentiation. For bispecifics, demonstrating superiority over the combination of two separate mAbs is the key value proposition that must be substantiated.

questions.question: Are trial designs (Ph1–Ph3) scientifically and statistically sound?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluation of trial design rigor. For biologics, Ph1 design must address: MABEL-based FIH dosing (for immune-activating biologics), step-up dosing schema (for bispecifics), patients-only Ph1 (most biologics cannot be tested in healthy volunteers), and dose optimisation per Project Optimus (for oncology biologics). Ph2/3 must address target expression-based enrichment and biomarker-driven adaptive designs.
questions.rationale: Biologic Ph1 design is fundamentally different from SM: most biologics go directly to patients, FIH doses for bispecifics are determined by MABEL (often 1000-fold below efficacious dose), step-up dosing is standard for T-cell engagers, and dose escalation may require 15-20 cohorts. FDA Project Optimus now expects OBD identification before pivotal trials.

questions.question: Are endpoints (primary, secondary, exploratory) clinically meaningful and validated?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluation of endpoint selection. For biologics, includes immune-mediated efficacy endpoints (objective response rate, complete response, target cell depletion), PD/biomarker endpoints (receptor occupancy, cytokine profiles, immune cell phenotyping), and patient-reported outcomes appropriate for biologic-specific AE profiles (CRS, infusion reactions).
questions.rationale: Biologic endpoints often include immune-mediated response measures and PD biomarkers that serve dual roles as efficacy indicators and safety monitors. For bispecifics, PD endpoints (T-cell activation, target cell depletion) are critical secondary endpoints that support the MOA narrative.

questions.question: Is the choice of patient population appropriate and justified?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluation of population selection. For biologics, includes target expression-based eligibility criteria (IHC scoring thresholds, gene expression cut-offs), CDx-defined enrollment, prior biologic therapy exposure status, and for bispecifics, assessment of whether patients can safely receive step-up dosing (adequate organ function, no active infections).
questions.rationale: Patient selection for biologics is often target expression-driven — patients whose tumours do not express the target cannot benefit. The cut-off for target-positivity directly impacts trial feasibility, response rates, and label scope. For bispecifics, eligibility must also ensure patients can safely tolerate the CRS risk inherent in immune engagement.

questions.question: Are inclusion/exclusion criteria optimised for recruitment and generalisability?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluation of I/E criteria balance. For biologics, includes assessment of whether biologic-specific exclusions are appropriate: prior biologic exposure washout, autoimmune disease exclusions (for immune-modulating biologics), viral hepatitis screening (for immunosuppressive biologics), and tumour accessibility for biopsy (if target expression confirmation is required).
questions.rationale: Biologic-specific exclusion criteria (autoimmune history, chronic infections, prior biologic exposure) can significantly impact recruitment feasibility. Overly restrictive criteria may generate a trial population that does not reflect the real-world treatment population, limiting label scope and payer acceptance.

questions.question: Are comparator arms (placebo, active) aligned with standard of care?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluation of comparator selection. For biologics, includes assessment of whether the comparator is the current biologic SOC (which may be another mAb or bispecific), whether biosimilar versions of the SOC are available and acceptable as comparators, and whether combination comparator arms (e.g., chemotherapy + biologic SOC) are appropriate.
questions.rationale: In many biologic indications, the SOC is another biologic — comparator selection must account for biologic access (approved biosimilars may be preferred for cost), and whether the comparator adequately tests the differentiation hypothesis. For bispecifics claiming superiority over mAb combinations, the combination arm must be appropriately designed.

questions.question: For bispecifics and immune-engaging biologics: is the dose escalation design appropriate, including step-up dosing, CRS monitoring, and site readiness requirements?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether the FIH dose escalation incorporates bispecific-specific design elements: MABEL-based starting dose, intra-patient step-up dosing schedule, mandatory CRS monitoring period (typically 24-48h post-first dose), cytokine monitoring panels, CRS grading scale (ASTCT or Lee criteria), tocilizumab availability at sites, and site qualification criteria ensuring CRS management capability.
questions.rationale: Bispecific FIH trials require specialised design elements that do not exist for SM or conventional mAb trials. Step-up dosing (multiple escalating doses within a single patient before reaching target dose) is now standard for T-cell engagers. Sites must be qualified for CRS management with trained staff, monitoring capability, and immediate access to tocilizumab. These operational requirements fundamentally shape trial design and site selection.

questions.question: Is the dose optimisation strategy aligned with FDA Project Optimus expectations for identifying the optimal biological dose?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether the trial programme identifies the OBD rather than just the MTD. Assessment of whether randomised dose comparison is planned before or within pivotal trials, whether PK/PD/biomarker data support the selected dose, and whether dose expansion cohorts at multiple dose levels are included.
questions.rationale: FDA Project Optimus (final guidance August 2024) expects oncology sponsors to identify OBD before pivotal trials. For biologics — where target saturation often occurs well below MTD — this is particularly relevant. Programmes that advance a single dose to pivotal without dose optimisation evidence face increasing regulatory risk.

questions.question: Are completed studies conducted under GCP with complete CSRs?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether studies are GCP-compliant with finalised CSRs.
questions.rationale: GCP compliance and complete CSRs form the evidentiary backbone of regulatory submissions.

questions.question: Are data quality metrics acceptable?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether quality indicators meet standards.
questions.rationale: Data quality metrics are increasingly scrutinised as surrogates for study reliability.

questions.question: Are data sources verifiable and audit-ready?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether data systems meet regulatory expectations.
questions.rationale: Data integrity is fundamental to regulatory acceptance.

questions.question: Have any data integrity issues been flagged?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether past issues are documented and resolved.
questions.rationale: Unresolved data integrity issues can trigger rejection.

questions.question: Is a Safety Management Plan (SMP) and Medical Monitoring Plan in place?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether safety management is structured. For biologics, SMP must address biologic-specific safety risks: CRS/ICANS monitoring and grading protocols (for bispecifics), infusion reaction management algorithms, immunogenicity-related safety monitoring, immune-mediated AE recognition and management (irAEs for checkpoint-related biologics), and infection monitoring (for immunosuppressive biologics).
questions.rationale: Biologic safety management requires disease- and mechanism-specific safety plans. CRS management protocols (grading, intervention thresholds, tocilizumab/corticosteroid algorithms) are mandatory for T-cell engaging bispecifics. Infusion reaction management is standard for IV biologics. Infection monitoring is critical for immunosuppressive mAbs.

questions.question: Are safety data actively reviewed (e.g., DSMB, internal safety committees)?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether active safety review occurs. For biologics, includes assessment of whether CRS/ICANS events are reviewed in real-time during dose escalation, whether immunogenicity-related safety events are tracked, and whether DSMB charter addresses biologic-specific stopping rules.
questions.rationale: For bispecifics, real-time safety review during dose escalation is critical — CRS events in early cohorts directly inform subsequent dosing decisions (step-up schedule, dose modifications, premedication requirements). DSMB charters must include biologic-specific stopping rules for CRS severity thresholds.

questions.question: Are AE, SAE, and SUSAR data adequately collected and reported?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether safety reporting is compliant. For biologics, includes biologic-specific AE coding considerations: CRS/ICANS as expected events with grading (not just SAE reporting), infusion-related reactions, immunogenicity-related AEs (injection site reactions from ADA, hypersensitivity), and immune-mediated organ toxicity.
questions.rationale: Biologic AE profiles differ from SM — CRS, ICANS, infusion reactions, and immunogenicity-related events require specific coding, grading, and management algorithms. Standard MedDRA coding may not capture the nuance of CRS grading; programmes should use validated grading scales (ASTCT, Lee criteria) alongside standard AE reporting.

questions.question: Are risk mitigation strategies defined for known or potential safety issues?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether risks are proactively mitigated. For biologics, includes CRS mitigation strategy (step-up dosing, premedication, hospitalisation requirements), infection prophylaxis (for immunosuppressive biologics), tumour lysis syndrome management (for haematologic biologics), and immunogenicity mitigation (concomitant immunosuppression, immune tolerance protocols).
questions.rationale: Biologic risk mitigation is mechanism-specific. Step-up dosing with premedication is now standard for T-cell engaging bispecifics. Anti-CD20 mAbs require PJP prophylaxis and hepatitis B screening. Tumour lysis syndrome prevention is required for haematologic indications. These are not general safety measures but biologic-specific clinical management requirements.

questions.question: For biologics: are sites qualified for biologic-specific safety management (CRS management capability, infusion infrastructure, immunogenicity monitoring)?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether clinical trial sites meet biologic-specific capability requirements: IV infusion suites with monitoring equipment, trained staff for CRS recognition and management, tocilizumab/corticosteroid availability on-site, resuscitation capability, overnight observation capability (for bispecific first doses), and laboratory capacity for real-time cytokine monitoring if required.
questions.rationale: Biologic trials — particularly bispecific FIH studies — require site capabilities beyond standard clinical trial infrastructure. Sites must be able to manage CRS events, which can progress rapidly to life-threatening severity. Site qualification for CRS management capability is a safety imperative and a practical operational requirement that directly impacts site selection and geographic distribution.

questions.question: Do early data support proof-of-concept for the intended indication?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether POC data are compelling. For biologics, includes immune-mediated POC signals (target cell depletion, immune activation markers, cytokine profiles) alongside traditional efficacy endpoints. For bispecifics, assessment of whether the obligate mechanism (dual-target-dependent efficacy) is demonstrated in patients.
questions.rationale: Biologic POC often rests on immune-mediated signals rather than traditional disease modification endpoints. For bispecifics, demonstrating that efficacy depends on simultaneous dual-target engagement (the obligate mechanism) is a critical POC milestone that differentiates the bispecific from its monospecific components.

questions.question: Is there evidence of dose-response and clinical effect?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether dose-response is characterised. For biologics, includes assessment of whether target coverage (receptor occupancy) correlates with response, whether a bell-shaped dose-response exists (bispecifics), and whether the OBD has been identified distinct from MTD.
questions.rationale: For biologics, dose-response is driven by target coverage rather than plasma concentration alone. For bispecifics, bell-shaped dose-response (higher doses reducing efficacy due to competitive inhibition of trimer formation) must be explicitly investigated. Project Optimus mandates OBD identification.

questions.question: Are endpoints aligned with regulatory expectations for approval and labeling?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether endpoints meet regulatory standards. For biologics, includes assessment of whether accelerated approval endpoints (ORR, CR rate for haematologic biologics) are appropriate, whether confirmatory endpoints for full approval are planned, and whether immune-mediated response criteria (iRECIST for immunotherapy) are used appropriately.
questions.rationale: Many oncology biologics (particularly bispecifics) pursue accelerated approval using surrogate endpoints (ORR, CR rate). Confirmatory studies must be planned prospectively. Immune-modified response criteria (iRECIST, Lugano for lymphoma) may be more appropriate than standard RECIST for biologics with immune-mediated mechanisms.

questions.question: Are any surrogate endpoints used appropriately and validated?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether surrogates are justified. For biologics, includes assessment of whether surrogate endpoints for accelerated approval (ORR, minimal residual disease, ctDNA clearance) have regulatory precedent in the biologic class and indication.
questions.rationale: Accelerated approval using surrogate endpoints is common for oncology biologics. The choice of surrogate must have regulatory precedent — FDA's acceptance of specific surrogates varies by indication and class. Emerging surrogates (MRD, ctDNA) are increasingly relevant for biologics but require agency alignment.

questions.question: Are KOLs engaged or consulted for trial design and positioning?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether KOLs are engaged. For biologics, includes engagement of KOLs with biologic-specific expertise (immunotherapy, bispecifics, CRS management), and assessment of whether KOL feedback informs biologic-specific design decisions (step-up dosing, biomarker-driven enrichment).
questions.rationale: KOL engagement for biologics requires experts with specific experience in biologic administration, immune-mediated AE management, and biomarker-driven patient selection. For bispecifics, KOLs experienced with approved T-cell engagers provide critical practical insight.

questions.question: Are publications, congress presentations, and medical communications planned?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether medical communications are planned.
questions.rationale: Timely and strategic medical communications are essential for clinical adoption and scientific credibility.

questions.question: Is there a plan for MSL engagement, payer education, or scientific advisory boards?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether stakeholder engagement is planned. For biologics, includes payer education on biologic value proposition (justifying premium pricing vs. SM alternatives or biosimilars), MSL training on biologic-specific clinical data and administration requirements, and advisory board focus on biologic-specific clinical questions.
questions.rationale: Biologic payer education requires specific value argumentation — justifying cost differential vs. SM or biosimilar alternatives, demonstrating clinical differentiation, and addressing administration burden (IV infusion time, SC self-injection training). MSLs must be trained on biologic-specific clinical pharmacology.

questions.question: Are real-world evidence (RWE) or post-marketing studies being considered?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether RWE strategies are planned. For biologics, includes immunogenicity monitoring in real-world populations, long-term safety in broader populations (infections, malignancies for immunosuppressive biologics), and effectiveness data to support payer value demonstration and biosimilar differentiation.
questions.rationale: RWE is particularly important for biologics to demonstrate long-term safety (immune-mediated risks may emerge with extended exposure), real-world effectiveness beyond controlled trial settings, and sustained value differentiation as biosimilar competition emerges.

questions.question: Have clinical programmes been discussed with regulators?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether regulatory interactions have occurred.
questions.rationale: Early regulatory engagement de-risks development.

questions.question: Are clinical endpoints and trial designs aligned with regulatory guidance?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether designs align with guidance. For biologics, includes alignment with FDA Project Optimus (dose optimisation), ICH S6(R1) (nonclinical-clinical bridging), FDA bispecific antibody guidance (FIH design, clinical pharmacology), and disease-specific guidances for biologic indications.
questions.rationale: Biologic-specific guidance documents (FDA bispecific guidance, Project Optimus, ICH S6(R1)) directly inform clinical design and must be explicitly integrated. Non-alignment is a common source of IND clinical holds and BLA deficiencies.

questions.question: Are accelerated designations pursued?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether expedited pathways are leveraged.
questions.rationale: Accelerated designations are critical for competitive positioning.

questions.question: Is the clinical module of the CTD (Module 5) complete or in preparation?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether Module 5 is being prepared.
questions.rationale: Module 5 readiness is essential for timely submission.

questions.question: Are enrollment timelines realistic and tracked?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether enrollment projections are realistic. For biologics, includes assessment of whether enrollment accounts for target expression screening (screen failure rates can be 50-70% for expression-selected populations), CDx availability at sites, and for bispecifics, the extended dose escalation period (which can require 15-20 cohorts over 2+ years).
questions.rationale: Biologic enrollment faces unique challenges: target expression-based screening creates high screen failure rates, CDx testing adds logistical complexity, and bispecific dose escalation can involve 15-20 dose cohorts over years. Enrollment projections that do not account for these biologic-specific factors are unreliable.

questions.question: Are CROs selected and performance-managed effectively?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether CRO oversight is adequate. For biologics, includes assessment of whether CROs have biologic-specific trial management experience (IV infusion studies, CRS monitoring, immunogenicity sample management, cold-chain investigational product handling).
questions.rationale: Biologic trial management requires CRO capabilities beyond standard SM trial execution — cold-chain IP management, IV infusion monitoring, CRS event management experience, and immunogenicity sample collection logistics. CROs without biologic trial experience may lack these capabilities.

questions.question: Is site selection optimised?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether site selection is data-driven. For biologics, includes assessment of IV infusion capability, CRS management readiness (for bispecifics), laboratory capacity for real-time biomarker monitoring, cold-chain IP storage, and geographic access to target-expression-positive patient populations.
questions.rationale: Biologic site selection requires assessment of capabilities that are irrelevant for SM trials — infusion suites, CRS management training, cold-chain storage, and laboratory infrastructure for immune monitoring. For bispecifics, site CRS management capability is a safety-critical selection criterion.

questions.question: Are protocol amendments frequent, and are their reasons understood?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether amendment frequency is tracked. For biologics, common biologic-specific amendments include: dose modification based on emerging CRS data, step-up dosing schedule revisions, biomarker cut-off adjustments, and manufacturing process change-driven protocol updates.
questions.rationale: Biologic trials, particularly bispecific dose-finding studies, inherently require more amendments than SM trials — dose escalation decisions, step-up schedule optimisation, and CRS management algorithm refinements are legitimate reasons for protocol evolution. Understanding which amendments reflect normal biologic development vs. poor planning is important.

questions.question: Is a SAP in place and consistent with the protocol?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether SAP is documented and aligned.
questions.rationale: The SAP is critical for scientific integrity and regulatory confidence.

questions.question: Are sample size calculations and power assumptions justified?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether powering is adequate. For biologics, includes consideration of high response rate expectations (for haematologic bispecifics), time-to-event endpoints with complex censoring (for immuno-oncology), and biomarker-stratified sample sizing.
questions.rationale: Biologic trials often require different sample size considerations: haematologic bispecifics may show very high response rates (>60%) requiring smaller samples, while immuno-oncology trials may need larger samples to capture delayed separation of survival curves.

questions.question: Is there an IDMC/DSMB if applicable?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether independent oversight is established.
questions.rationale: Independent safety oversight is essential for high-risk trials.

questions.question: Are interim analyses planned under appropriate controls?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether interims are properly governed.
questions.rationale: Interim analyses are powerful but must preserve integrity.

questions.question: Is the proposed product differentiated in efficacy, safety, convenience, or access?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether differentiation is substantiated. For biologics, includes differentiation on: mechanism (bispecific vs. mAb combination), administration convenience (SC vs. IV, dosing frequency), safety profile (CRS incidence/severity vs. competitor bispecifics), and target coverage (novel target vs. validated target in crowded space).
questions.rationale: Biologic differentiation increasingly focuses on format advantages (bispecific vs. combination), administration convenience (SC formulations, reduced infusion time), and safety differentiation (lower CRS rates, improved therapeutic index). These biologic-specific differentiation dimensions must be explicitly substantiated.

questions.question: Is the positioning clear vs. existing and emerging therapies?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether positioning is defined. For biologics, includes positioning vs. approved biologics, emerging bispecifics/ADCs, cell therapies, and biosimilars of established biologics.
questions.rationale: The biologic competitive landscape is evolving rapidly — with bispecifics, ADCs, and cell therapies competing for the same patient populations. Positioning must account for this multi-modality competitive dynamics.

questions.question: Have competitor trial designs, endpoints, and outcomes been benchmarked?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether competitive intelligence is systematic.
questions.rationale: Benchmarking is essential for realistic development strategy.

questions.question: Are benefit-risk assessments conducted?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether structured B-R exists.
questions.rationale: Effective B-R assessment is critical for regulatory and strategic clarity.

questions.question: Are there any clinical red flags that could impact approvability?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether risks are identified. For biologics, includes immunogenicity-related efficacy loss, CRS/ICANS severity signals, infection rates for immunosuppressive biologics, and manufacturing comparability concerns that could impact clinical data interpretation.
questions.rationale: Biologic-specific clinical red flags include: high-titre ADA with efficacy loss, unmanageable CRS severity, opportunistic infections, and process change-driven product quality shifts during pivotal trials. These require biologic-experienced clinical leadership to recognise and address.

questions.question: Are toxicity or safety signals manageable with monitoring, mitigation, or labeling?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether safety signals are manageable. For biologics, includes CRS manageability (grading, intervention, outcomes), immunogenicity impact manageability, and whether safety profile supports chronic administration (for non-oncology indications).
questions.rationale: For biologics, key safety manageability questions include: Is CRS predictable and treatable? Does immunogenicity lead to clinical consequences requiring discontinuation? Can infections be prevented with prophylaxis? These determine clinical viability.

questions.question: Are there defined "no-go" criteria for development continuation?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether termination thresholds are pre-agreed.
questions.rationale: Pre-defined no-go criteria prevent sunk-cost bias.

questions.question: Is there a lifecycle management plan?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether LCM is planned. For biologics, includes indication expansion strategy (common for mAbs/bispecifics), line extension to SC formulation, biosimilar defence strategy (after BPCIA exclusivity), and next-generation biologic development (improved format, enhanced potency).
questions.rationale: Biologic lifecycle planning differs from SM: indication expansion is often the primary growth driver (rather than formulation line extensions), SC conversion extends product life, and biosimilar competition (rather than generic) defines the competitive endpoint.

questions.question: Are post-approval study commitments or Phase 4 trials planned?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether post-approval commitments are resourced.
questions.rationale: Post-approval commitments are critical for maintaining market access.

questions.question: Are registries or real-world studies planned for value demonstration?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether RWE strategies support value.
questions.rationale: RWE is critical for sustained market access and reimbursement.

questions.question: Are all clinical study protocols, SAPs, CSRs, and patient narratives archived?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether documentation is complete.
questions.rationale: Robust documentation is essential for compliance and submissions.

questions.question: Are ICFs, ethics approvals, and investigator site files inspection-ready?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether files are inspection-ready.
questions.rationale: GCP compliance in documentation is a core regulatory obligation.

questions.question: Are all clinical modules of the eCTD populated and consistent?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether eCTD clinical sections are complete.
questions.rationale: Module 5 completeness is essential for submission readiness.

questions.question: Is the clinical leadership experienced and credible?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether leadership has relevant experience. For biologics, includes assessment of whether clinical leadership has biologic-specific development experience (immunotherapy, bispecifics, CRS management, biomarker-driven development), not just general oncology or therapeutic area expertise.
questions.rationale: Biologic clinical development requires specialised expertise in immune-mediated pharmacology, CRS management, biomarker-driven patient selection, and dose optimisation methodologies (MABEL, step-up dosing) that differ from SM development. Leadership without this experience may make suboptimal clinical design decisions.

questions.question: Is medical oversight provided by board-certified physicians in the relevant area?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether medical oversight is qualified.
questions.rationale: Qualified medical oversight is a regulatory expectation.

questions.question: Are clinical trial governance and decision-making processes documented?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether governance is structured and traceable.
questions.rationale: Documented governance ensures transparent and defensible decision-making.

questions.question: Is immunogenicity monitoring prospectively integrated into the clinical programme with pre-defined decision criteria?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether immunogenicity monitoring is prospectively planned with sampling timepoints aligned to PK sampling, ADA status-stratified efficacy/safety analyses pre-specified in the SAP, and decision criteria defined for dose adjustment, treatment discontinuation, or switch based on ADA impact.
questions.rationale: Immunogenicity monitoring for biologics is a regulatory requirement — but its value depends on prospective integration into clinical decision-making, not just retrospective reporting. Pre-specified ADA-stratified analyses and decision criteria enable real-time clinical management and support regulatory confidence in the immunogenicity narrative.

questions.question: Are immunogenicity-related clinical events (infusion reactions, loss of response, hypersensitivity) prospectively tracked and correlated with ADA status?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether clinical events potentially related to immunogenicity are prospectively captured and correlated with ADA testing results. Assessment of whether infusion/injection reactions, hypersensitivity events, loss of clinical response, and PK changes are systematically analysed by ADA status.
questions.rationale: Clinical immunogenicity manifests through specific event types — infusion reactions (often ADA-mediated), loss of response (neutralising ADA), and hypersensitivity (immune complex formation). Correlating these events with ADA status is essential for understanding the clinical impact of immunogenicity and for constructing the immunogenicity narrative in labeling.

questions.question: Is a clinical immunogenicity risk mitigation strategy in place (e.g., concomitant immunosuppression, immune tolerance induction, SC vs. IV route optimisation)?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether clinical strategies to reduce immunogenicity risk or mitigate its impact are defined: concomitant immunosuppression (e.g., methotrexate for anti-TNF mAbs), IV loading before SC maintenance (to establish immune tolerance), dose/regimen optimisation to reduce ADA incidence, and treatment algorithms for ADA-positive patients.
questions.rationale: For biologics where immunogenicity significantly impacts clinical outcomes, mitigation strategies can substantially improve the benefit-risk profile. Concomitant methotrexate reduces ADA incidence for multiple mAbs; IV loading before SC transition can reduce immunogenicity risk. These strategies must be evidence-based and integrated into the clinical programme design.

questions.question: Is the administration regimen (IV/SC, dosing frequency, infusion time) optimised for the target treatment setting and patient population?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether the administration regimen is appropriate for the clinical context: outpatient vs. inpatient administration, infusion time (shorter infusion = better patient experience and site throughput), SC self-administration feasibility, dosing frequency impact on patient burden, and whether step-up dosing requires hospitalisation or can be managed in outpatient settings.
questions.rationale: Biologic administration burden directly impacts clinical adoption, site capacity, patient adherence, and commercial success. Reducing infusion time (e.g., from 4h to 30min through ramp-up protocols), enabling SC self-administration, and reducing dosing frequency are competitive differentiators that must be optimised during clinical development — not post-approval.

questions.question: Are patient-reported outcomes (PROs) capturing biologic-specific treatment burden (infusion time, injection site reactions, CRS symptoms)?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether PROs are designed to capture biologic-specific patient experience: infusion-related symptoms, injection site reactions (for SC products), CRS symptom burden (for bispecifics), impact of dosing frequency on quality of life, and self-administration confidence/preference. Assessment of whether PRO instruments are validated for the biologic patient population.
questions.rationale: PRO instruments designed for oral SM administration may not capture the relevant treatment burden for biologics. Infusion time, injection pain/reactions, CRS recovery period, and the psychological burden of frequent hospital visits are biologic-specific dimensions of patient experience that must be measured to support differentiation and payer value claims.

questions.question: For combination regimens: is the clinical trial design appropriate to evaluate the contribution of each component and the combination benefit-risk?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether combination trial design addresses: scientific rationale for the combination (mechanistic complementarity), contribution of each component to combination efficacy (factorial or randomised add-on design vs. single-arm combination), combination-specific safety monitoring (overlapping toxicities, additive immune activation), and regulatory expectations per FDA co-development guidance. For bispecifics combined with checkpoint inhibitors, assessment of whether overlapping CRS and irAE risks are characterised.
questions.rationale: Biologics are increasingly developed in combination — bispecifics with checkpoint inhibitors, mAbs with chemotherapy, dual biologics. FDA expects evidence of each component's contribution to the combination. For bispecifics + CPI combinations, overlapping immune activation may amplify both efficacy and toxicity. The combination trial design must prospectively address these issues — retrofitting after single-agent development is costly and time-consuming.

questions.question: Are resistance mechanisms characterised and is the treatment sequencing strategy defined?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether biologic-specific resistance mechanisms are prospectively investigated: antigen loss/downregulation (target escape), T-cell exhaustion (for immune-engaging biologics), immune checkpoint upregulation, tumour microenvironment remodelling, and ADA-mediated loss of efficacy. Assessment of whether the programme has a strategy for sequencing relative to other available biologics (prior anti-CD20 → bispecific, prior CPI → bispecific) and whether retreatment/rechallenge after prior biologic therapy is studied.
questions.rationale: Biologic resistance mechanisms differ fundamentally from SM resistance — target antigen loss, immune exhaustion, and immune evasion are the dominant mechanisms. Understanding these informs treatment sequencing (which biologic after another fails), retreatment strategies (can the same biologic be restarted), and next-generation molecule design. Treatment sequencing is becoming a critical clinical question as the number of approved biologics in the same indication grows.

questions.question: Are long-term immune safety risks (infections, hypogammaglobulinaemia, secondary malignancies) characterised and monitored?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether long-term immune consequences of biologic therapy are prospectively assessed: infection risk from sustained immunosuppression (PJP, HBV reactivation, opportunistic infections), hypogammaglobulinaemia from B-cell depleting agents (IgG monitoring, IVIG replacement criteria), secondary malignancy risk from chronic immune modulation, and autoimmune events from checkpoint-related biologics. Assessment of whether monitoring duration extends beyond the treatment period.
questions.rationale: Long-term immune safety is a uniquely biologic concern. B-cell depleting agents (anti-CD20, BCMA-targeting bispecifics) cause prolonged hypogammaglobulinaemia requiring IgG monitoring and potentially IVIG replacement. Chronic immune modulation creates theoretical secondary malignancy risk. These long-term consequences may not manifest during pivotal trials but must be prospectively planned for in post-marketing and registry studies.

questions.question: If accelerated approval is pursued: is the confirmatory study strategy defined prospectively with regulatory agreement?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether the confirmatory study (required to convert accelerated to full approval) is prospectively designed, initiated, and agreed with FDA before or at the time of accelerated approval. Assessment of whether the confirmatory endpoint is clinically meaningful (OS, PFS), whether enrollment is feasible in the post-approval setting, and whether there is a timeline commitment that satisfies FDA's FDORA requirements for accelerated approval confirmatory studies.
questions.rationale: Many oncology biologics (particularly bispecifics) receive accelerated approval based on surrogate endpoints (ORR, CR rate). Under FDORA (2022), FDA has enhanced authority to require and enforce confirmatory study timelines. Failure to complete confirmatory studies has led to voluntary withdrawals of accelerated approvals. Prospective confirmatory study planning is now a regulatory and commercial imperative — not an afterthought.

questions.question: Are investigators and site staff trained on biologic-specific clinical management (CRS recognition/grading, infusion reaction management, immunogenicity-related event identification)?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether a structured investigator training programme addresses biologic-specific clinical management: CRS recognition and grading (ASTCT criteria), CRS intervention algorithms (tocilizumab, corticosteroids, vasopressors), ICANS recognition and grading, infusion reaction management protocols, when to hold/restart dosing after immune events, and immunogenicity-related clinical event identification (loss of response, hypersensitivity). Assessment of whether training is documented, refreshed, and required before site activation.
questions.rationale: Biologic trials — particularly bispecific T-cell engager studies — require investigators with specific training in immune event management that goes beyond standard AE reporting. CRS can progress from mild to life-threatening within hours; untrained investigators may undergrade CRS, delay intervention, or fail to recognise ICANS. Structured investigator training on biologic-specific safety management is a patient safety imperative.

questions.question: Is the biomarker/CDx-driven screening process operationalised with acceptable screen failure rates and turnaround times?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether target expression-based screening is operationally implemented: central vs. local testing strategy, CDx assay turnaround time relative to screening window, screen failure rate monitoring and mitigation, archival vs. fresh biopsy requirements, and whether the screening process accounts for heterogeneous target expression. Assessment of whether screen failure rates are tracked and whether enrollment projections account for the screening attrition.
questions.rationale: Biomarker-driven screening is standard for many biologic programmes (HER2 IHC for trastuzumab, PD-L1 for pembrolizumab, target expression for bispecifics). Screen failure rates can reach 50-70% for stringent expression cut-offs, fundamentally impacting enrollment feasibility. CDx turnaround times (often 5-10 business days) can exceed screening windows, causing patient drop-out. These operational realities must be prospectively managed.

questions.question: Is a treatment interruption and retreatment strategy defined for dose delays or treatment breaks?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether the protocol and clinical guidance address: criteria for dose delay vs. discontinuation, retreatment after planned or unplanned treatment breaks (re-step-up dosing for bispecifics), immune reconstitution assessment before retreatment (for immunosuppressive biologics), and PK/PD implications of treatment interruption (loss of steady-state, ADA development during drug-free interval).
questions.rationale: Treatment interruptions are common in biologic therapy (due to AEs, intercurrent illness, surgical procedures). For bispecifics, restarting after a break may require repeating step-up dosing to avoid CRS upon re-exposure. For immunosuppressive biologics, immune reconstitution assessment may be needed. Treatment interruption strategy is a critical clinical guidance question that is often inadequately addressed in protocols.

questions.question: Is the product positioned relative to the rapidly evolving biologic competitive landscape, including next-generation formats (bispecifics, ADCs, cell therapies)?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether competitive positioning accounts for the full biologic competitive landscape: approved mAbs and biosimilars, competing bispecifics (same target, different format), ADCs targeting the same antigen, cell therapies (CAR-T) in the same indication, and emerging multispecific formats (trispecifics, tetraspecifics). Assessment of whether the differentiation strategy addresses format-specific advantages and limitations.
questions.rationale: The biologic competitive landscape is evolving faster than any other therapeutic space — with bispecifics, ADCs, cell therapies, and now trispecific/tetraspecific agents all competing for the same patient populations. Positioning must account for this multi-modality competition. A bispecific must differentiate not only vs. mAb combinations but also vs. CAR-T (which may offer deeper responses but with different safety/access profiles) and ADCs (which may offer better solid tumour penetration).
